name: Tu
description: Tu, also known as Tūmatauenga, is a major god in Māori mythology. He is the god of war, humanity, hunting, cooking, and fishing. He represents the fierce and aggressive aspects of life. In the primordial darkness, Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother) were locked in a tight embrace, preventing light and life from entering the world. Their children, the gods, grew frustrated with the confinement. Tūmatauenga was the most resolute of the brothers and proposed that they kill their parents to bring forth light. While some, like Tāwhirimātea (god of storms), vehemently opposed this, others, like Tāne (god of forests and birds), favored separating them. Tū's proposal to kill their parents was not carried out, but he was instrumental in Tāne's successful effort to separate Rangi and Papa. After Tāne separated their parents, Tāwhirimātea, enraged by his brothers' actions, attacked them, forcing them to seek refuge. Tūmatauenga, however, stood firm and retaliated, eventually subduing all his brothers: Tangaroa (god of the sea), Tāne, Rongomātāne (god of cultivated plants), and Haumia-tiketike (god of wild food plants). Because of this, Tūmatauenga is considered the ancestor of humanity, who, in Māori tradition, dominate and consume these other elements of the natural world. His dominance over his brothers is a central theme in many Māori stories, reflecting humanity's relationship with the environment. He is often depicted with a fearsome expression and is associated with rituals and practices related to warfare and hunting. He is a powerful and complex figure, embodying both the creative and destructive forces of nature.
subtitle: God of war, humanity, and aggression

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Tū proposed killing his parents to bring light to the world, showcasing his resolute and aggressive nature. Ultimately, Tāne separated them, but Tū's willingness to resort to violence is significant.

keyMyths.title: Tū's Domination of His Brothers
keyMyths.description: After Tāwhirimātea attacked his brothers, Tū subdued them, establishing humanity's dominance over the natural world. This reflects the Māori worldview of humans utilizing and consuming the environment.
keyMyths.source: Reed, Myths and Legends of Maoriland

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: Tūmatauenga is considered the ancestor of humanity because of his ability to stand against and overcome his brothers. This makes him the progenitor of the dominant species on Earth.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, The Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend

sources.author: Sir George Grey

sources.text: A foundational text on Māori mythology, providing detailed accounts of the gods and their stories.
